Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd occasionally think about sitting down to work, but most of the time I just didn't get around to it. When I did study, it was usually late at night when I couldn't sleep, and I'd listen to some lecture recordings while doing other stuff. There wasn't any real structure to it.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, and I wouldn't even say deadlines motivated me that much. Sometimes a deadline would pass and I'd just shrug it off. I had other things going on - work stuff, hanging out with people from my extracurriculars - and studying just kept sliding down the priority list. Regular study times weren't really my thing.
Q3: Usually I'd log in because I got some notification email or because I vaguely remembered something was due. Sometimes I'd log in just to see what I was missing, scroll around for a bit, then close it. It wasn't like I had a purpose most times - more like checking in out of guilt or boredom.
Q4: Not really, no. It never became routine for me. I'd go weeks without logging in and then suddenly remember the course existed. The mindfulness stuff they had me try was supposed to help me focus, but I just couldn't make the LMS a regular part of my day. It always felt like an afterthought.
Q5: The lecture recordings were probably the most useful because I could listen to them while doing other things. I'm better at picking things up when I hear them rather than reading. The videos were okay too, I watched a decent amount of those. But honestly, even the useful stuff didn't feel that engaging to me.
Q6: The discussion boards were basically useless to me - I posted once and that was it. Reading materials I'd ignore completely because staring at text just doesn't work for how I learn. The assignments piled up and I kept telling myself I'd get to them, but I rarely did. It all just felt like too much effort for something I wasn't invested in.
Q7: If something felt quick and didn't require a ton of mental energy, I'd maybe do it. Activities that let me passively absorb information, like listening to lectures, felt more worth my time. But honestly, not much felt truly worth it this semester. I kept asking myself why I was even bothering with this course.
Q8: Required stuff felt like a burden I was supposed to carry but kept putting down. Optional stuff? I didn't even consider it most of the time. If it wasn't going to directly affect my grade, there was zero chance I was doing it. Even the required things barely got my attention, so optional was completely off the table.
Q9: Yeah, sometimes I'd download a lecture and listen to it on my phone while commuting or at work. I wouldn't go back to the LMS to show I'd engaged with it. I also talked to a few classmates about course stuff during our extracurricular activities, which obviously doesn't show up anywhere online.
Q10: There were times I'd have a lecture playing in my earbuds while I was at my part-time job or hanging out. I was technically studying but the LMS would have no idea. Also, some of my peer interactions happened in person, not through the platform, so that learning was invisible too.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download audio files or save PDFs and then never open the LMS again for that material. I preferred having stuff on my phone where I could access it without dealing with the platform. Once it was downloaded, I didn't see the point in logging back in.
Q12: Pretty much the whole semester was hard to engage with, if I'm being real. I just felt bored with the content and couldn't muster up the energy to care. Around midterms I had some extra shifts at work and my extracurricular stuff picked up, so the course just faded into the background completely.
Q13: Fatigue was a big one - I was tired a lot and the course felt like the easiest thing to let slide. Competing responsibilities definitely played a role too. When I had to choose between the course and literally anything else, the course lost every time. Confusion about what I was supposed to be doing didn't help either.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd have videos playing but I'd be scrolling on my phone or zoning out completely. I'd technically be logged in and watching something, but my brain was somewhere else entirely. It was just going through the motions without actually absorbing anything.
Q15: All the time. I knew assignments were important but I'd keep pushing them off, telling myself I'd do them tomorrow. I think I just didn't care enough to prioritize them. There was always something more interesting or less demanding to do instead, and the consequences felt distant.
Q16: Deadlines would sometimes make me log in and panic-scroll through materials, but I rarely actually submitted anything. They created temporary stress but not enough to make me follow through. After missing a few, I kind of gave up on caring about them altogether. They lost their power over me pretty quickly.
Q17: I barely got any feedback because I barely submitted anything. The little feedback I did receive didn't really change my behavior. By that point I'd already mentally checked out of the course. It's hard to be motivated by feedback when you're not engaged enough to produce work in the first place.
Q18: The structure felt rigid and didn't match how I learn. Everything was set up for people who read and write, but I'm more of a listener. The weekly modules felt repetitive and boring. If the structure had been more flexible or audio-focused, maybe I would've participated more.
Q19: The heavy reliance on written materials made it harder for me. Having to navigate through multiple clicks to find anything was annoying. The discussion requirements felt forced and pointless. On the easier side, having recorded lectures helped, but that was about the only design element that worked for me.
Q20: I think some people might be logging in and clicking around without actually doing the work, kind of like what I did. You can watch videos and browse materials without ever completing an assignment. High activity doesn't mean productive activity - it might just mean someone's lost or procrastinating.
Q21: That doesn't really reflect my experience since I had low activity AND performed poorly. But I can see how someone might study effectively offline and just show up for the important stuff. If you're efficient and know what you're doing, you probably don't need to live on the LMS.
Q22: Course logs can show when you clicked on things and how long you had them open, but they can't show if you were actually paying attention. They miss offline studying, conversations with classmates, and whether someone is genuinely understanding the material. A lot of real learning happens away from the screen.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and wanting to understand it. It's not about logging in or clicking through modules - it's about mental investment. This semester, I just didn't have that. I was physically present sometimes but mentally I'd already drifted away.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I was dealing with a lot of boredom and just general disinterest in the subject matter. It wouldn't capture the conversations I had with peers outside the platform or the audio lectures I listened to on my commute. It also can't show that I was going through a period where I just couldn't bring myself to care about academics.
